coredata.dc:title: L-asparaginase-mediated pH shift and carbon dot fluorescence modulation: A sensitive ratiometric method for quantifying L-asparagine in diverse potato varieties under variable storage conditions

coredata.dc:description: This study presents a novel and selective method for the determination of l-asparagine in diverse potato varieties under various storage conditions. L-asparagine levels serve as a crucial indicator for acrylamide formation, a hazardous substance in processed potato products. The fluorometric method utilized blue-emitting CDs (B-CDs), orange-emitting CDs (O-CDs), and the enzyme L-asparaginase for ratiometric detection of L-asparagine. Upon enzymatic hydrolysis of L-asparagine by L-asparaginase, liberated ammonia induced a pH increase in the reaction medium. This pH shift enhanced the fluorescence of B-CDs while simultaneously decreasing that of O-CDs, enabling sensitive and selective L-asparagine quantification. Comprehensive characterization of the CDs was performed using various spectroscopic techniques and transmission electron microscopy. The method demonstrated excellent sensitivity (LOD = 0.31 μM) and a wide linear range (1.0–50.0 μM). When the method was applied to potato samples, high recovery values (98.00–100.33 %) with low relative standard deviations (RSDs) were achieved, confirming the accuracy and precision of the method. The approach was employed to determine L-asparagine levels in three potato varieties (Lady Rosetta, Spunta, and Nicola) under different storage temperatures and durations. This method provides a valuable tool for monitoring L-asparagine content in potatoes, potentially aiding in the mitigation of acrylamide formation during processing. The robust performance and simplicity of the proposed technique make it suitable for routine analysis in both research and industrial applications within the potato industry.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents a novel and selective method for the determination of l-asparagine in diverse potato varieties under various storage conditions. L-asparagine levels serve as a crucial indicator for acrylamide formation, a hazardous substance in processed potato products. The fluorometric method utilized blue-emitting CDs (B-CDs), orange-emitting CDs (O-CDs), and the enzyme L-asparaginase for ratiometric detection of L-asparagine. Upon enzymatic hydrolysis of L-asparagine by L-asparaginase, liberated ammonia induced a pH increase in the reaction medium. This pH shift enhanced the fluorescence of B-CDs while simultaneously decreasing that of O-CDs, enabling sensitive and selective L-asparagine quantification. Comprehensive characterization of the CDs was performed using various spectroscopic techniques and transmission electron microscopy. The method demonstrated excellent sensitivity (LOD = 0.31 μM) and a wide linear range (1.0–50.0 μM). When the method was applied to potato samples, high recovery values (98.00–100.33 %) with low relative standard deviations (RSDs) were achieved, confirming the accuracy and precision of the method. The approach was employed to determine L-asparagine levels in three potato varieties (Lady Rosetta, Spunta, and Nicola) under different storage temperatures and durations. This method provides a valuable tool for monitoring L-asparagine content in potatoes, potentially aiding in the mitigation of acrylamide formation during processing. The robust performance and simplicity of the proposed technique make it suitable for routine analysis in both research and industrial applications within the potato industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: L-asparagine, a non-essential amino acid, plays a crucial role in the biosynthesis of glycoproteins and plant development (Ali et al., 2016). It has garnered significant attention due to its involvement in the formation of acrylamide, a potentially harmful compound, during the cooking of food products like potatoes (Muttucumaru et al., 2017). Acrylamide is a chemical compound that forms primarily when foods rich in carbohydrates are cooked at high temperatures, such as during frying, roasting, or baking. L-asparagine reacts with reducing sugars (like glucose and fructose) present in food through the Maillard reaction to produce acrylamide, especially when exposed to high heat (Zhu et al., 2010). This reaction is notably significant in potatoes, which are rich in both L-asparagine and sugars. Acrylamide is not only a product of industrial processes but also a byproduct of the cooking process at home or in commercial settings (Pęksa et al., 2021). Classified as a potential human carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer, it has raised concerns about dietary exposure through commonly consumed foods (Amrein et al., 2003). Foods such as potatoes, coffee, and cereal products, which undergo high-temperature processing, are major contributors to dietary acrylamide intake. The formation of acrylamide in potatoes is primarily dependent on the cooking method and temperature (Plata-Guerrero et al., 2009). High-temperature cooking methods, such as frying, convert L-asparagine and sugars present in potatoes into acrylamide more efficiently compared to methods like boiling or microwaving (Amrein et al., 2003). The concentration of acrylamide in food products can be influenced by several factors, including the content of precursor L-asparagine and sugars (Bachir et al., 2023). Some studies indicate that the L-asparagine content in potatoes is a more critical factor than sugar levels in acrylamide formation, emphasizing the importance of its control and monitoring (Amrein et al., 2003). Factors such as potato variety, growing conditions, and storage conditions can also significantly impact the levels of these precursors (Muttucumaru et al., 2017).

Monitoring L-asparagine levels in food products, such as potatoes, is crucial for predicting and managing acrylamide levels. By assessing the L-asparagine content, food producers can make informed decisions regarding the choice of potato varieties, cultivation practices, and optimal cooking methods to minimize acrylamide formation. This proactive approach not only enhances food safety but also aids in compliance with regulatory standards aimed at reducing dietary acrylamide exposure. Such monitoring also contributes to broader efforts in agricultural practices, breeding programs, and food processing technologies aimed at producing healthier food products.

Determining L-asparagine concentrations involves different methods, each with unique advantages and limitations that impact their practical applications. Flow-injection analysis (FIA) offers rapid analysis and high sample throughput, while capillary electrophoresis (CE) provides excellent resolution for separating multiple amino acids. Both methods are versatile and can be automated for consistent results. FIA approaches (Stein et al., 1996) are particularly useful for quick screening of large sample sets. Capillary electrophoresis with UV detection excels in separating multiple amino acids simultaneously, offering good sensitivity and requiring small sample volumes (Omar et al., 2017). Spectrophotometric methods based on inhibition effects (Labrou & Muharram, 2016) or chemiluminescence (Kugimiya & Fukada, 2015) reactions provide high throughput and sensitivity, and are often cost-effective. High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) methods especially those using diethyl ethoxymethylenemalonate derivatization, offer comprehensive profiling of multiple amino acids and biogenic amines with high accuracy and precision (Gómez-Alonso et al., 2007). Ratiometric fluorescent biosensors provide real-time, continuous monitoring capabilities with high sensitivity (An et al., 2017). Continuous flow procedures allow for automated, high-throughput analysis (Ahmad et al., 2017). Enzyme-based methods offer high specificity for L-asparagine and can be adapted for in-situ measurements (Labrou & Muharram, 2016).

However, these methods also present various limitations. FIA and CE methods can be complex, time-consuming, and often fail to detect trace levels of L-asparagine in complex biological matrices due to sensitivity limitations; additionally, FIA approaches may be affected by interfering compounds in food samples. CE with UV detection requires a derivatization step with 4-chloro-7-nitro-2, 1, 3-benzoxadiazole (NBD-Cl), which adds complexity and potential variability to the analysis. Spectrophotometric methods might be susceptible to interference from other amino acids or compounds in food samples that react similarly. Specifically, assays using Nessler's reagent may react with other substances present in samples, leading to inaccurate results. HPLC methods are time-consuming, require expensive equipment, and often involve complex sample preparation, such as a 30-min derivatization step followed by 2 h of heating, making them less suitable for rapid, routine testing. Ratiometric fluorescent biosensors and some FIA methods have restricted linear ranges, limiting their applicability to real samples. Continuous flow procedures may have insufficient sensitivity for trace analysis. Enzyme-based methods often struggle with long-term stability of immobilized enzymes, potentially necessitating frequent recalibration or replacement, and can be labor-intensive to set up. The ideal method would combine high selectivity for L-asparagine, rapid analysis time, simple sample preparation, and robust performance across various food matrices, but achieving all these criteria simultaneously remains a significant challenge in the field.

Carbon dots (CDs) are nanoscale particles, typically smaller than 10 nm, composed primarily of carbon (Alanazi, Alhazzani, Mostafa, Barker, El-Wekil, & Ali, 2024). They are known for their excellent photoluminescent properties, including a high quantum yield and stability under photo-irradiation (Ozyurt et al., 2023). CDs offer several advantages such as low toxicity, environmental friendliness, and versatile chemical modifiability, making them suitable for a wide range of applications including bioimaging, drug delivery, sensors, and optoelectronics (Alhazzani, Alanazi, Mostafa, Barker, El-Wekil, & Ali, 2024c). They can be prepared through various methods, including microwave-assisted synthesis, hydrothermal synthesis, and electrochemical etching, which allow for controlled size and functional properties (Alhazzani, Alanazi, Mostafa, Barker, El-Wekil, & Ali, 2024b). Doping CDs with heteroatoms like nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus is crucial as it significantly enhances their optical and electronic properties. Nitrogen doping, for instance, introduces new energy levels in the electronic structure of CDs, enhancing their luminescence and conductivity. Sulfur and phosphorus doping similarly improve charge carrier mobility and quantum efficiency (Alyami et al., 2023; Mahmoud et al., 2024b). These dopants also increase the CDs' chemical reactivity and selectivity towards specific targets, boosting their performance in catalysis and sensing applications (Mahmoud et al., 2024a). Thus, heteroatom doping not only tailors the properties of CDs for specific applications but also expands their utility in various scientific and industrial fields.

This novel fluorometric method for detecting L-asparagine levels in potato samples represents a significant advancement in food quality analysis and acrylamide formation prediction. The approach uniquely combines L-asparaginase enzyme activity with the pH-sensitive fluorescence properties of two types of CDs: blue-emitting (B-CDs) and orange-emitting (O-CDs). When L-asparaginase catalyzes the hydrolysis of L-asparagine, it liberates ammonia, which causes an increase in pH. This pH change triggers differential responses in the fluorescence intensities of the two types of CDs: the B-CDs increase in intensity, while the O-CDs decrease. This inverse relationship enabled a ratiometric detection strategy, which offers improved accuracy and precision compared to single-emission methods. By directly correlating the fluorescence ratio to L-asparagine concentration, this technique provides a rapid and sensitive means to quantify L-asparagine in potato samples. This allows for the prediction of potential acrylamide formation during high-temperature cooking processes. This innovative approach overcomes many limitations of previous methods, offering a simpler, faster, and potentially more cost-effective solution for routine L-asparagine monitoring in the food industry. It eliminates the need for complex sample preparation or expensive analytical equipment (Alhazzani, Alanazi, Mostafa, Barker, El-Wekil, & Ali, 2024a).

L-asparaginase (lyophilized powder, 100 units/mg of protein) isolated from E. coli was obtained from Merck, Darmstadt, Germany. L-asparagine, ethanol, sucrose, urea, 1, 4-benzoquinone, o-phenylenediamine, phosphoric acid and sodium hydroxide were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Sodium chloride, potassium chloride, magnesium chloride, calcium chloride, zinc chloride, copper chloride, sodium fluoride, sodium iodide, sodium carbonate, sodium bicarbonate, sodium sulphate, sodium sulphite, disodium hydrogen phosphate, glutamic acid, aspartic acid, ascorbic acid, proline, citric acid, chlorogenic acid, leucine, glutamine, histidine, acrylamide, lysine, malic acid, glucose, fructose, and sucrose were purchased from El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co., Cairo, Egypt. Double distilled water (DDW) from Aquatron Automatic Water Still apparatus (Model 4 L/Hr) was used in all aqueous solutions.

The optical properties of the CDs were investigated using fluorescence and ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer, with samples excited at 350 nm and 5 nm slit widths. Absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu 1601 PC UV–Vis spectrophotometer. To elucidate the structural and morphological characteristics of the CDs, several analytical techniques were employed. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a JEOL 2100F instrument at 200 kV accelerating voltage, providing insights into the size and shape of the nanoparticles. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using a Philips PW 1700 diffractometer to assess the crystalline structure. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted on KBr pellets using a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer, revealing information about functional groups present on the CDs' surface. Surface chemical composition and bonding states were analyzed through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB250 spectrometer. This comprehensive suite of characterization techniques provided a detailed understanding of the optical, structural, and chemical properties of the CDs, enabling a thorough evaluation of their potential for sensing applications.

B-CDs were synthesized using a previously reported environmentally friendly, oil-bath method with slight modifications (Liu et al., 2017). The precursors, sucrose (10 g) and urea (2 g), were combined in 20 mL of edible oil and heated to 250 °C. The reaction mixture was subjected to mechanical agitation for 5 min, during which a color transition from white to dark brown was observed, indicating the formation of B-CDs. Upon cooling to ambient temperature, 30 mL of DDW was introduced to facilitate phase transfer of the hydrophilic B-CDs into the aqueous medium. The biphasic system was then subjected to liquid-liquid extraction using a separation funnel to isolate the aqueous phase containing the B-CDs. The oil phase was retained for potential reuse in subsequent syntheses. The aqueous solution of B-CDs was subjected to lyophilization and subsequently stored at a temperature of 4 °C for subsequent characterization and application studies.

O-CDs were synthesized following previously established procedures with slight modifications (Pal et al., 2019). To synthesize O-CDs, 30 mg of 1,4-benzoquinone and 30 mg of o-phenylenediamine were dispersed in 20 mL of water via 30 min of sonication. Subsequently, 5 mL of phosphoric acid was added dropwise to the reaction mixture, which was then stirred under reflux for 8 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the mixture was redispersed in 10 mL of deionized water and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was dialyzed with a 1 kDa membrane for 12 h and lyophilized to obtain purified O-CDs as a fine powder.

The quantum yield (QY) of the CDs was determined using quinine sulfate (QS) as a reference standard (Hu & Zhang, 2013). The calculation was performed according to the following methodology: quinine sulfate, dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4, served as the reference fluorophore with a known quantum yield of 54 %. Both the CDs and QS solutions were prepared at concentrations that yielded absorbance values below 0.05 to minimize inner filter effects and ensure linearity of the fluorescence response. The absorbance and emission spectra of the CDs and QS were measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The integrated fluorescence intensities and absorbance values were then used in the following equation to calculate the quantum yield of the CDs:
ϕ CDs
=
ϕ QS
×
F CDs
F QS
×
A QS
A CDs
×
η CDs
η QS

In this context, ΦCDs denotes the quantum yield of the CDs, while ϕQS represents the quantum yield of quinine sulfate (QS). FCDs and FQS correspond to the fluorescence intensities of the CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. The term A refers to the absorbance value and η indicates the refractive index of the solvent. For this experiment, the CDs were dissolved in distilled water with a refractive index of η = 1.33, and quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4, also with a refractive index of η = 1.33.

The assay involved combining 0.1 mL of the purified L-asparaginase enzyme solution (100.0 U/L) with 0.9 mL of 1.0 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) and 1 mL of different concentrations of L-asparagine solution. This mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 10 min to facilitate the enzymatic reaction. Subsequently, the reaction was terminated by rapidly cooling the mixture in an ice bath and immediately adding 1.0 mL of ice-cold 99 % (v/v) ethanol. The reaction contents were then subjected to centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 15 min. After centrifugation, the supernatant was collected, and 1.0 mL of the supernatant was diluted with distilled water to a final volume of 8.0 mL. To this diluted mixture, 1.0 mL of B-CDs (5.0μg mL−1) and 1.0 mL of O-CDs (25.0μg mL−1) were introduced. The reaction was allowed to proceed for 1.0 min before measuring the fluorescence emission upon excitation at 350 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of L-asparagine from potato samples
ce:section.ce:para: Potatoes from three different varieties (Spunta, Lady Rosetta, and Nicola) were acquired from a local supermarket. Potato samples were processed into a fine powder using a household food blender. The method for extracting free L-asparagine was adapted from (Ohara-Takada et al., 2005a). Specifically, 3.0 g of the homogenized potato was placed in a 50 mL centrifuge tube, to which 10 mL of 80 % ethanol was added. The mixture was shaken vigorously for one minute and then subjected to amino acid extraction three times using a mechanical shaker at room temperature for an hour each session. Following this, the mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for five minutes. The clear liquid above the sediment was then filtered through two layers of filter paper. The clear supernatant of this filtrate was put into a glass vial and evaporated under a gentle flow of nitrogen gas until dryness. The dry residue was then dissolved in DDW to a final volume of 3.0 mL, and subsequently analyzed according to the methods previously outlined at Section 2.5. The accuracy of the analytical method was evaluated using the standard addition technique. Aliquots of 1.0 mL from previously prepared sample solutions were subjected to a 100-fold dilution, followed by the addition of known concentrations of L-asparagine at multiple levels (1.0, 3.0 and 6.0 μM). These spiked samples, along with unspiked controls, were analyzed using the developed method. The percentage recovery was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of L-asparagine levels under different storage conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Three potato varieties (Spunta, Lady Rosetta, and Nicola) were selected for the determination of L-asparagine levels under varying storage conditions. Fresh tubers of each variety were initially analyzed for L-asparagine content (0 months), and then subjected to two different storage temperatures: 2 °C and 5 °C. Samples were analyzed at three time points: 0, 2, and 4 months of storage. For each variety, temperature, and time point combination, triplicate samples were prepared and analyzed. L-asparagine levels were quantified using the previously designed method (Section 2.6), and results were expressed in μmol/kg fresh weight. The storage chambers were maintained at constant temperatures (±0.5 °C) and relative humidity (85–90 %) throughout the experiment. Tubers were randomly selected for analysis at each time point to ensure representative sampling.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the prepared B-CDs and O-CDs reveal distinct characteristics. The B-CDs are nearly spherical with minor variations in particle size and are mono-dispersed, as depicted in the TEM images (Fig. 1A). The inset of Fig. 1A highlights that the mean particle size of these B-CDs is approximately 5.8 nm. Conversely, the O-CDs also exhibit a near uniform particle size distribution and are mono-dispersed, ensuring consistency in their form (Fig. 1B). The inset of Fig. 1B specifies that the average particle size for the O-CDs is 3.4 nm. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) data for the synthesized B-CDs and O-CDs indicate distinct characteristics of their structural compositions. Both types of CDs display broad peaks in their XRD patterns, with B-CDs showing a peak at 25.7° (Fig. S1A) and O-CDs at 23.18° (Fig. S1B) (Mahmoud et al., 2024a, 2024b). These broad peaks are indicative of the amorphous nature of the CDs. The absence of sharp crystalline peaks confirms that both B-CDs and O-CDs lack a well-ordered crystalline structure, which is typical for amorphous materials (Mahmoud et al., 2024a). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of the CDs revealed distinct elemental compositions for B-CDs and O-CDs. The B-CDs exhibited three primary peaks at 288.65 eV, 402.12 eV, and 533.42 eV, which can be attributed to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s core levels, respectively (Fig. 1C). In contrast, the O-CDs displayed a more complex XPS spectrum with four distinct peaks (Fig. 1D). The peaks at 534.67 eV corresponding to O 1 s, 403.81 eV assigned to N 1 s, and 285.25 eV characteristic of C 1 s (Alyami et al., 2023). The peak at 135.11 eV likely corresponds to P 2p, indicating the successful incorporation of phosphorus from phosphoric acid into the O-CD structure.

The full XPS spectrum of B-CDs (Fig. S2) show a typical carbon dot structure with CO (288.56 eV), CN/CO (285.81 eV), and CC/CC (284.23 eV) bonds. Their nitrogen spectrum indicates the presence of NHCO/pyridine-N (399.83 eV) and NH2/pyrrolic-N (398.90 eV), while the oxygen spectrum shows CO (533.77 eV), CO (532.89 eV), and OH (531.76 eV) bonds, suggesting a surface rich in oxygen-containing functional groups. In contrast, O-CDs exhibit a slightly different carbon structure (Fig. S3) with CO at a higher binding energy (289.26 eV). Their nitrogen spectrum uniquely features CN/pyridinic-N (398.78 eV), NH2/pyrrolic-N (400.23 eV), and NH3 + (402.56 eV), indicating the presence of protonated amino groups. Notably, O-CDs also contain phosphorus, with PC (133.85 eV) and PO (133.08 eV) bonds, and their oxygen spectrum shows PO (530.59 eV), CO/PO (531.79 eV), and CO/POH (533.59 eV) bonds (Rooj & Mandal, 2023).

The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis of B-CDs provides valuable insights into their chemical structure (Fig. 1E). The broad peak at 3444 cm−1 is indicative of OH stretching vibrations, which suggests the presence of multiple hydroxyl groups (Alqahtani et al., 2024). The peak at 1750 cm−1 can be attributed to CO stretching vibrations. The 1627 cm−1 peak points to CC stretching in aromatic structures, suggesting the formation of conjugated systems. The peak at 1107 cm−1 represents CO stretching (Rooj & Mandal, 2023). Additionally, the peak at 1011 cm−1 likely corresponds to COC linkages. Finally, the 626 cm−1 peak might signify out-of-plane bending vibrations of aromatic CH groups, supporting the presence of aromatic rings. The FTIR analysis of O-CDs reveals distinct absorption peaks that suggest specific functional groups and structural components (Fig. 1E) (Alhazzani et al., 2023). The broad peak at 3438 cm−1 indicates OH/NH stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1640 cm−1 and 1570 cm−1 are likely due to CO stretching in amides and NH bending in primary amines, respectively. The peak at 1442 cm−1 could represent CH bending. The peak at 986 cm−1 might be associated with PO stretching, indicating the presence of phosphorus from the phosphoric acid treatment. Additionally, the peak at 877 cm−1 is suggestive of aromatic CH out-of-plane bending, and the peak at 781 cm−1 could be attributed to CH bending vibrations in alkenes or aromatic structures (Alhazzani, Alanazi, Mostafa, Barker, Ibrahim, et al., 2024d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic characterization of B-CDs and O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The spectroscopic properties of the CDs reveal distinctive characteristics for both B-CDs and O-CDs. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of B-CDs exhibits three prominent peaks at 236 nm, 280 nm, and 420 nm (Fig. 2A), which peaks can be attributed to π-π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds (236 nm), n-π* transitions (280 nm), and surface state transitions (420 nm), respectively (Zhang et al., 2021). The fluorescence emission spectrum of B-CDs shows a maximum at 430 nm when excited at 347 nm. Notably, the B-CDs demonstrate excitation-dependent emission behavior, a common feature of CDs, as evidenced by the spectral changes observed when varying the excitation wavelength from 300 to 380 nm (Fig. 2B) (Alaseem et al., 2024). This phenomenon is typically attributed to the presence of multiple emission centers or a distribution of different-sized nanoparticles within the sample (Sharma et al., 2016). The UV–visible absorption spectrum of O-CDs displays two prominent peaks: one at 265 nm and another at 427 nm (Fig. 2C). The peak at 265 nm can be attributed to π-π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds, while the peak at 427 nm likely corresponds to n-π* transitions of surface functional groups, possibly involving oxygen-containing moieties or an extended conjugated system. In the fluorescence spectrum, O-CDs exhibit a maximum emission at 520 nm when excited at 349 nm. Interestingly, O-CDs demonstrate excitation-independent emission behavior when the excitation wavelength is scanned from 300 to 380 nm (Fig. 2D). This excitation-independent property suggests a more uniform distribution of emissive states or particle sizes in O-CDs, contrasting with the excitation-dependent behavior observed in B-CDs (Wen & Yin, 2016). The quantum yield of the B-CDs is 32.5 %, while the O-CDs have a quantum yield of 17.5 %.

The photoluminescent properties of the synthesized B-CDs and O-CDs were extensively evaluated under diverse environmental conditions to assess their stability and potential applicability. The pH sensitivity tests revealed contrasting behaviors between the two types of CDs (Fig. S4A). B-CDs exhibited a gradual enhancement in fluorescence intensity as the pH increased from acidic to basic conditions, potentially due to alterations in surface chemistry or charge states. In contrast, O-CDs displayed a decline in photoluminescence with increasing pH, suggesting different surface interactions or electronic transitions. The effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence properties was investigated by exposing both types of CDs to NaCl solutions ranging from 0.01 to 2.0 mol L−1 (Fig. S4B). Surprisingly, neither B-CDs nor O-CDs showed significant changes in their fluorescence intensities across this concentration range, indicating remarkable stability in various ionic environments (Alhazzani, Alanazi, Mostafa, Barker, Ibrahim, et al., 2024e). The resistance to ionic strength variations suggests potential applications in complex biological or environmental matrices where salt concentrations may fluctuate. To evaluate their photostability, both types of CDs were subjected to prolonged UV irradiation for up to 520 min (Fig. S4C). Remarkably, neither B-CDs nor O-CDs exhibited any noticeable decrease in fluorescence intensity throughout this extended exposure period. This exceptional resistance to photobleaching underscores their potential for long-term imaging or sensing applications where sustained luminescence under continuous illumination is crucial (Alanazi, Alhazzani, Mostafa, Barker, Ibrahim, et al., 2024).

To maximize the efficiency of ammonia liberation from L-asparagine by L-asparaginase, we systematically investigated several key parameters. The pH dependency was examined across a range of 4.0–11.0 (Fig. S5A), with the highest fluorescence ratio observed at pH 7.5, indicating optimal conditions for the enzyme-substrate interaction. L-asparaginase concentration was evaluated from 1.0 to 1000.0 U/L (Fig. S5B). The fluorescence ratio peaked at 100.0 U/L and remained constant thereafter, suggesting this concentration as the most effective for reaction completion. We explored the impact of temperature between 20 and 80 °C, finding that 37 °C yielded the highest ammonia liberation, thus identifying it as the optimal reaction temperature (Fig. S5C). The enzymatic reaction duration was assessed from 1.0 to 30.0 min, with 10 min determined as sufficient for complete L-asparagine degradation and maximum ammonia release (Fig. S5D). Additionally, we investigated the effect of incubation time after adding both types of CDs, testing intervals from 0.5 to 10 min. Interestingly, just 1 min of incubation was sufficient to produce the optimal response, indicating that the CDs rapidly enhance the L-asparagine-L-asparaginase reaction (Fig. S5E). Finally, the optimal conditions were determined to be: pH 7.5, L-asparaginase concentration of 100.0 U/L, temperature of 37 °C, enzymatic reaction duration of 10 min, and CD incubation time of 1 min.

The sensitivity and dynamic range of B-CDs/O-CDs/L-asparaginase system were evaluated by exposing it to L-asparagine concentrations from 1.0 to 50.0 μM under optimized conditions. As L-asparagine levels increased, we observed a corresponding decline in the O-CDs' fluorescence at 520 nm, while the B-CDs' emission at 430 nm experienced a gradual enhancement (Fig. 3A). The ratiometric analysis of these fluorescence intensities (F430/F520) revealed a strong linear correlation with L-asparagine concentration across the entire tested range (Fig. 3B). This relationship is described by the equation F430/F520 = 0.0312C(L-asparagine) + 0.135, with an impressive correlation coefficient of 0.9984. Our method achieved a detection limit of 0.31 μM, demonstrating its capability for precise L-asparagine quantification in food samples. When compared to traditional analytical techniques such as colorimetry, chemiluminescence, chromatography, electrophoresis, and various flow injection methods, our approach shows significant advantages for a broad-range L-asparagine detection. Table 1 provides a comprehensive comparison of detection limits across different methodologies, underscoring the competitive performance of our system. While some existing methods exhibit comparable detection range, they often face limitations as discussed earlier in the introduction, highlighting the unique benefits of our innovative approach. The combination of a wide linear range, low detection limit, and the advantages inherent to our dual CDs system positions this method as a promising tool for L-asparagine analysis in complex food matrices.

Our innovative CDs-based L-asparagine detection system (B-CDs/O-CDs/L-asparaginase) underwent rigorous testing to assess its selectivity and resistance against potential interferents commonly found in potato matrices. This evaluation is crucial, as accurate L-asparagine quantification in potatoes serves as a key indicator for predicting acrylamide formation rates during high-temperature processing. We conducted comprehensive fluorescence spectral analyses in the presence of various compounds at 1.0 mM, encompassing a wide range of potential interferents: amino acids (e.g., glutamine, proline, lysine, and serine), organic acids (such as aspartic acid, glutamic acid, citric acid, ascorbic acid, malic acid, and chlorogenic acid), metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+), other biological molecules (glucose, fructose, sucrose, and acrylamide itself), and various anions (Cl−, F−, I−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, HPO4 2−). As illustrated in Figs. S6A and S6B, the fluorescence ratio signals remained largely unaffected by these diverse potato matrix components. This exceptional selectivity towards L-asparagine, even in complex potato environments, underscores the system's potential for reliable L-asparagine quantification in potato samples. The consistency of fluorescence signals amidst potential interferents demonstrates the robustness of our method, suggesting its viability as a highly specific analytical tool for assessing acrylamide formation potential in potato-based products. This capability is particularly valuable for food quality control and safety monitoring in the potato processing industry, where accurate prediction of acrylamide formation is crucial for minimizing this potentially harmful compound in final products.

The enzymatic hydrolysis of L-asparagine by L-asparaginase produces ammonia as a by-product, leading to an increase in pH and subsequent alkalinization of the reaction medium. This pH shift has a profound impact on the optical properties of the synthesized CDs. The surface characteristics of these nanoparticles are modified due to the protonation or deprotonation of their amino and carboxyl moieties in response to the changing pH environment. As the solution becomes more alkaline, the B-CDs exhibit an increase in their emission intensity. Conversely, the O-CDs display an opposite trend, with their luminescence intensity decreasing as the pH rises.

The observed increase in fluorescence intensity can be explained by the deprotonation process occurring on the surface of the B-CDs. As the pH of the environment rises, the carboxylic acid groups present on the B-CDs' surface undergo deprotonation, transforming into carboxylate anions. This transformation results in the formation of a negatively charged layer enveloping the B-CDs. This electrostatic shell serves a crucial function by inhibiting the non-radiative recombination of photo-excited states within the B-CDs (Gong et al., 2015). Consequently, this inhibition leads to an enhancement of the B-CDs' fluorescent emission intensity. To unravel the mechanism behind the enhanced fluorescence of B-CDs in alkaline conditions, we conducted a series of in-depth analyses. TEM revealed a reduction in B-CD particle aggregation upon L-asparagine addition, with average sizes decreasing from 5.8 nm to 2.28 nm (Fig. 4A and B). This size stability in the presence of L-asparagine suggests that the fluorescence enhancement stems from electrostatic repulsion between negatively charged CDs. Zeta potential measurements further corroborated this hypothesis, showing a shift from positive to negative values as L-asparagine concentrations increased from 1.0 to 50.0 mM (Fig. S7A). Collectively, these findings paint a comprehensive picture of the fluorescence enhancement mechanism, highlighting the crucial role of electrostatic interactions and surface chemistry changes in the B-CDs' response to alkaline conditions induced by L-asparagine hydrolysis.

The XPS analysis of B-CDs offers valuable insights into the surface chemistry changes occurring under alkaline conditions (Fig. S8), supporting the proposed mechanism of fluorescence enhancement. For carbon, we observe a shift in the CO peak from 288.56 to 288.19 eV, accompanied by a decrease in intensity. The CN/CO peak shifts from 285.81 to 285.52 eV with a slight increase in intensity, while the CC/CC peak moves from 284.23 to 283.90 eV, and its intensity remains relatively stable. In the oxygen spectrum, the CO peak shifts from 533.77 to 533.47 eV with a decrease in intensity, the CO peak slightly moves from 532.89 to 532.92 eV with a slight increase in intensity, and the OH peak shifts from 531.76 to 531.66 eV with a decrease in intensity. The nitrogen peaks remain relatively stable in both position and intensity. These changes, particularly the shifts and intensity changes in the oxygen-containing groups, indicate surface deprotonation processes. The decrease in CO and OH intensities, coupled with the increase in CO intensity, suggests the conversion of carboxylic acid groups to carboxylate ions. This transformation likely contributes to the formation of a negatively charged surface layer, which could inhibit non-radiative recombination of photo-excited states, thereby enhancing fluorescence intensity (Rooj & Mandal, 2023).

The behavior of O-CDs in response to L-asparagine addition was investigated using TEM imaging and zeta potential measurements. At pH 7.0, TEM analysis revealed well-dispersed O-CDs with an average size of 3.4 nm. However, the introduction of L-asparagine induced aggregation of the O-CDs (Fig. 4 C and D). Zeta potential measurements showed a decline in positive surface charge with increasing L-asparagine concentrations (Fig. S7B), indicating the presence of protonated NH3 + groups at a low pH value. This transition from dispersed to aggregated states under varying L-asparagine concentrations leads to fluorescence quenching, highlighting the potential of O-CDs as L-asparagine-sensitive probes. Furthermore, at higher pH levels, the deprotonation of surface functional groups on O-CDs may induce electrostatic alterations, affecting their Fermi level and consequently modulating their fluorescence properties (Lin et al., 2016).

The XPS analysis of O-CDs offers valuable insights supporting the proposed mechanism of fluorescence quenching (Fig. S9). In the carbon spectra, the CO peak shifts slightly from 289.26 to 289.14 eV while remaining relatively stable in intensity. The CN/CO peak shows a minor shift from 285.25 to 285.35 eV with a slight decrease in intensity, while the CC/CC peak at 284.71 eV remains stable in both position and intensity. These subtle changes suggest that while the core structure of O-CDs remains largely intact, there are minor surface modifications occurring. The nitrogen spectra, showing peaks for CN/pyridinic-N (398.78 eV), NH2/pyrrolic-N (400.23 eV), and NH3 + (402.56 eV), are particularly informative. While specific data on how these peaks change with pH are not available, the presence of the NH3 + peak at lower pH aligns with the observed positive surface charge in zeta potential measurements. As pH increases with L-asparagine addition, these protonated groups likely undergo deprotonation, which would explain the decline in positive surface charge. The XPS spectra for oxygen atoms in O-CDs showed no significant changes after increasing pH, suggesting that these bonding environments remain relatively stable during the pH-induced transformations (Rooj & Mandal, 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the method to potato samples
ce:section.ce:para: The efficacy of the fluorometric method was evaluated through the quantification of free L-asparagine in three distinct potato cultivars: Lady Rosetta, Spunta, and Nicola. To assess the reliability and accuracy of the analytical system when applied to actual potato matrices, the standard addition method was employed. Recovery studies were conducted on the three potato varieties, with each sample spiked with standard L-asparagine at three different concentration levels. The recovery rates for L-asparagine in the fortified samples ranged from 98.00 % to 100.33 %, indicating high accuracy and minimal matrix effects. Furthermore, the precision of the method was demonstrated by the relative standard deviation (RSD) values, which were consistently below 3.21 % for each spiked concentration level (n = 3), as presented in Table 2 . These results suggest that the method exhibits satisfactory accuracy and precision for the quantitative determination of free L-asparagine in diverse potato cultivars. The method's performance characteristics support its reliability for accurately quantifying free L-asparagine levels in potato samples, which is crucial for assessing acrylamide formation potential and optimizing processing parameters. The proposed method was compared with an established chemiluminescent technique (Kugimiya & Fukada, 2015) for L-asparagine determination. The results obtained from both methods revealed no significant difference, thereby confirming the reliability and accuracy of the newly developed approach for quantifying L-asparagine in potato samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of storage time and temperature on L-asparagine levels in different potatoes varieties
ce:section.ce:para: L-asparagine levels (μmol/g) were determined in three different potato varieties (Lady Rosetta, Spunta, and Nicola) under different storage temperatures (2 °C and 5 °C) and storage times (0, 2, 4 months) (Fig. 5 ). At the start of the storage period (0 months), Lady Rosetta showed the highest initial L-asparagine content (25.2 μmol/g), followed by Spunta (20.1 μmol/g) and Nicola (15.7 μmol/g). When stored at 2 °C, all three varieties exhibited a more pronounced increase in L-asparagine levels compared to storage at 5 °C. After 2 months at 2 °C, Lady Rosetta reached 30.9 μmol/g (32.7 % increase), Spunta increased to 25.3 μmol/g (29.3 % increase), and Nicola rose to 20.7 μmol/g (27.0 % increase). After 4 months at 2 °C, Lady Rosetta displayed a further rise, reaching 35.9 μmol/g, a 51.9 % increase from its initial value. Spunta and Nicola also saw substantial increases, reaching 30.1 μmol/g (48.8 % increase) and 25.4 μmol/g (45.9 % increase), respectively. Storage at 5 °C resulted in a more moderate accumulation of L-asparagine across all varieties. By the 2-month mark at 5 °C, Lady Rosetta increased to 30.2 μmol/g (19.2 % increase), Spunta reached 20.8 μmol/g (17.1 % increase), and Nicola rose to 20.2 μmol/g (13.5 % increase). After 4 months at 5 °C, Lady Rosetta accumulated lower levels compared to 2 °C storage, reaching 30.8 μmol/g, a 30.8 % increase from its initial value. Spunta and Nicola followed a similar trend, with final concentrations of 25.3 μmol/g (29.3 % increase) and 20.6 μmol/g (24.3 % increase), respectively (Ohara-Takada et al., 2005b). The mean L-asparagine levels in various potato samples ranged from 15.7 μmol/g to 35.9 μmol/g fresh weight. These findings align with the reported values for L-asparagine in potato samples documented in the existing literature (Muttucumaru et al., 2017). The rate of L-asparagine accumulation was not uniform throughout the storage period. For all varieties and both storage temperatures, the increase was more rapid during the first two months compared to the latter two. This suggests that the biochemical processes responsible for L-asparagine synthesis or degradation may be more active in the early stages of storage. These results indicate that both storage temperature and duration significantly influence L-asparagine accumulation in potatoes, with lower temperatures leading to greater increases. The data reveal that different potato varieties have varying propensities for L-asparagine accumulation, with Lady Rosetta consistently showing the highest levels and the most dramatic increases over time. These findings have important implications for potato storage practices, particularly in relation to the potential formation of acrylamide during high-temperature cooking processes. Lady Rosetta, often used for chip production, showed the highest L-asparagine levels, which could be a concern for processors. The results suggest that careful management of storage temperature is crucial, with higher temperatures (within safe storage limits) potentially helping to minimize L-asparagine accumulation, especially for varieties like Lady Rosetta that are more prone to high L-asparagine levels. The differences observed between varieties could possibly be attributed to genetic factors influencing L-asparagine metabolism. Nicola, showing the lowest L-asparagine accumulation, might be a preferable choice for longer storage periods or when lower acrylamide potential is desired. However, other factors such as texture, taste, and overall suitability for specific culinary applications should also be considered when selecting potato varieties for different purposes. These results underscored the importance of variety-specific storage strategies in the potato industry, balancing the need for long-term storage with the management of L-asparagine levels to ensure food quality and safety. The study highlights the complex interplay between storage temperature, duration, and potato variety in determining L-asparagine content, emphasizing the need for tailored storage solutions in the potato industry.

The proposed fluorometric method represents a significant advancement in the detection and quantification of L-asparagine in potato varieties under various storage conditions. This analytical approach is especially valuable given the critical role of L-asparagine as a precursor to acrylamide, a potentially carcinogenic compound formed during high-temperature cooking of starchy foods. At the core of this method is the innovative use of two types of CDs (B-CDs and O-CDs) in conjunction with the enzyme L-asparaginase. The mechanism relies on the enzymatic reaction between L-asparagine and L-asparaginase, which liberates ammonia and consequently alters the pH of the system. This pH change triggers opposite fluorescence responses in the B-CDs and O-CDs, enabling a ratiometric detection strategy. This study reveals that both storage temperature and duration significantly affect L-asparagine accumulation in potatoes, with cooler temperatures increasing these levels. Among the varieties tested, Lady Rosetta exhibited the highest L-asparagine concentrations, posing a concern for its use in chip production due to potential acrylamide formation during cooking. The findings suggest that managing storage temperature could help reduce L-asparagine accumulation, particularly in varieties like Lady Rosetta. Conversely, the Nicola variety, which showed the lowest L-asparagine levels, might be preferable for longer storage or situations where lower acrylamide potential is desired. The research underscores the need for variety-specific storage strategies to balance long-term storage needs with food quality and safety, highlighting the complex interaction between the storage conditions and potato variety on L-asparagine content.
